title: Where You Come From and Who You Belong To

required.title: [Read Aloud] Little House in the Big Woods
required.author: Laura Ingalls Wilder

required.title: [Read Aloud] Family stories from the student's own family

competence_markers.description: The child can tell their family's story to the extent they know it
The child understands family roles and can describe their own contribution
The child actively contributes to the household — observed over time
The child understands the concept of intergenerational legacy

narration_prompts.detail: How far back can you go? What stories do you know about your grandparents or great-grandparents?

narration_prompts.prompt: What does each role in a family contribute?
narration_prompts.detail: What is your role right now?

narration_prompts.prompt: How do you take care of people in your family?
What does it mean to be a link in a chain?
narration_prompts.detail: What have you received from the people before you? What do you want to build for the people after you?

cross_connections.description: Family is commitment in daily action. The promise-keeping practiced in 4.4.6 sustains family life.
Contributing to the family requires the self-governance (chores, patience, kindness) built in IC1.
Families are how civilizations perpetuate themselves. The historical awareness from 1.1 provides context.
Running a household is the practical infrastructure of family life.

content.why_this_matters:
You belong to a family. This family has a story — a story that started before you were born and will continue after you are gone. You are part of something bigger than yourself.

Your grandparents worked to give your parents what they have. Your parents are working to give you what you need. And someday, you will work to give your children what they need. This chain — people giving to the people who come after them — is how families survive and how the good things in life get passed from one generation to the next.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Family as identity** — understanding that you belong to a story bigger than yourself
2. **Family roles** — fathers, mothers, grandparents, siblings, and what each contributes
3. **Care for others** — practicing responsibility through helping at home
4. **Stories of great families** — models of what family can be from history and literature
5. **The concept of legacy** — you receive from those who came before; you build for those who come after
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Your Family's Story

Every family has a story. Some families know their story well — they have photographs, letters, family trees, and stories passed down through generations. Some families have lost parts of their story through moves, through wars, through hardship. But every family has one.

Your story includes the people who came before you — your parents, your grandparents, your great-grandparents, and beyond. These people worked, struggled, loved, built, lost, and persevered so that you could be here. You may not know all their names. But their lives are the reason yours exists.

**A family tree project:** Draw your family tree as far back as you can. Ask your parents and grandparents (if available) to fill in names, places, and stories. Where were your grandparents born? What did they do for work? What was their life like? What stories do they remember from their own childhood?

The more of your family's story you know, the stronger your sense of identity. You are not a disconnected individual floating in the present. You are a link in a chain that extends backward through generations and forward through the family you will someday build.

### Part 2: Roles in a Family

Every family member has a role — a way they contribute to the family's life and well-being.

**Fathers** provide, protect, lead, and teach. A good father works to support his family, shields them from danger, makes decisions for the family's good, and teaches his children what they need to know. A father's presence is one of the most important factors in a child's development.

**Mothers** nurture, manage, care, and teach. A good mother provides the emotional warmth and daily care that hold a family together, manages the household's operations, tends to children's physical and emotional needs, and teaches through constant, loving attention. A mother's presence in early childhood shapes emotional development more than any other single factor.

**Grandparents** carry wisdom, tell stories, and provide the long view. They have seen more of life than you or your parents. Their experience — their successes and failures — is a resource that many families underutilize.

**Siblings** are your first peers. Living with brothers and sisters teaches sharing, negotiation, conflict, loyalty, and the reality that you are not the center of the universe. Siblings are both your first rivals and your first allies.

**You** are part of this team. Your role right now is to contribute — by helping with chores, by caring for younger siblings, by being kind to the people you live with, and by learning everything your family has to teach you. The more you contribute now, the more capable you will be when it is your turn to lead a family.

### Part 3: Caring for Others

One of the most important things you can practice right now is caring for others — not because you have to, but because it is how families work. Everyone helps. Everyone contributes. No one just receives.

Practical ways to care:
- Help with household chores without being asked
- Be kind to your siblings, especially when it is hard
- Help care for pets or younger children
- Visit elderly relatives and listen to their stories
- Notice when someone in your family is having a hard day and do something small to help

Every time you care for someone else, you are practicing the skill that will make you a good parent, a good spouse, and a good community member someday. Care is a muscle. Build it now.

### Part 4: The Chain of Generations

Here is the biggest idea in this packet: **you are in the middle of a chain.**

The people who came before you — your parents, grandparents, great-grandparents — worked and sacrificed to give you what you have. The education you are receiving, the home you live in, the values you are being taught — all of these came from someone's effort.

The people who will come after you — your children, your grandchildren, perhaps your great-grandchildren — will receive what you build. The character you develop, the skills you acquire, the family you create, the values you transmit — all of these will shape people who have not been born yet.

You are not building just for yourself. You are building for the future. The person who understands this — who sees themselves as a link in a chain, not an isolated individual — makes different choices. Better choices. Choices that consider not just "what do I want right now?" but "what will the people who come after me need?"

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Family Story Knowledge
**Developing well:** The child can tell their family's story — names, places, and at least a few specific stories about grandparents or great-grandparents. They show curiosity about family history and ask questions.
**Developing slowly:** The child shows little interest in family history or cannot recall basic information about their immediate family's background.
**Diagnostic:** Can the child, when prompted, narrate at least three facts about their family that go beyond the immediate household? If not, more storytelling and family conversation is needed.

### 2. Understanding of Family Roles
**Developing well:** The child can describe what fathers, mothers, grandparents, and siblings contribute to the family. They understand that each person has a role and that every role matters.
**Developing slowly:** The child sees family members only in terms of what they provide to the child personally, not in terms of their contribution to the family as a whole.
**Diagnostic:** Ask the child: "What does your [father/mother/grandmother] do for our family?" A child developing well will name multiple contributions — not just "buys me things" but "works to take care of us," "teaches me," "makes sure we are safe."

### 3. Active Household Contribution
**Developing well:** The child regularly helps with age-appropriate tasks — setting the table, feeding animals, picking up toys, helping with younger siblings — without needing to be asked every time. They show pride in contributing.
**Developing slowly:** The child resists household tasks, treats them as punishment, or contributes only when directly supervised and reminded.
**Diagnostic:** Observe over two weeks. Does the child initiate any household contribution without being asked? If yes, the habit is forming. If contribution only occurs under direct instruction, focus on working alongside the child and building the habit gradually.

### 4. Intergenerational Awareness
**Developing well:** The child can express, in their own words, the idea that people who came before them worked so they could have what they have, and that someday they will work for the people who come after them. They understand the concept of "being in the middle of a chain."
**Developing slowly:** The child thinks of themselves as the endpoint — a receiver only, not a future giver. The concept of building for the next generation has not yet taken root.
**Diagnostic:** Ask: "Who worked so that you could have what you have?" and "Who will you be working for someday?" A child developing well will reference parents, grandparents, and future children. A child developing slowly will be confused by the second question.
